shortDescription: Laser vision correction that reshapes the cornea to reduce dependence on glasses and contacts.

sections.overview:
LASIK is a laser refractive procedure that gently reshapes the cornea so that light focuses correctly on the retina, reducing or removing the need for glasses or contact lenses. Patients choose Korea because its clinics combine high-volume refractive expertise with modern femtosecond and excimer laser platforms, transparent pricing, and care pathways built for international visitors.

The procedure corrects common refractive errors such as nearsightedness, farsightedness, and astigmatism. A thin flap is created in the surface of the cornea, the underlying tissue is reshaped with a cool ultraviolet laser, and the flap is repositioned to heal naturally without stitches. Because the cornea has few nerve endings once numbed with drops, most patients feel only mild pressure during the few minutes the laser is active.

Korean eye centers typically map the cornea with detailed topography and wavefront scanning before treatment, allowing the laser pattern to be tailored to the individual eye. Same-trip planning is common: the screening, surgery, and first follow-up can often be arranged within a short stay, which suits patients traveling from abroad.

Vision usually begins clearing within hours, and many people return to routine activities the next day. The stated success rate of 95 percent or higher reflects how often patients reach their target vision range, though final results depend on the starting prescription and individual healing. A thorough consultation confirms whether LASIK, a surface procedure, or a lens-based option is the better fit for each set of eyes.
sections.candidacy:
Good candidates are generally adults whose prescription has been stable for at least a year, with healthy eyes free of active infection, significant dry eye, or conditions such as keratoconus. Corneal thickness is a central factor: the laser removes a small amount of tissue, so the cornea must be thick enough to retain a safe, stable structure afterward. This is measured during screening, and patients with thinner corneas may be guided toward a surface-based or lens-based alternative instead.

The screening also reviews pupil size, tear film quality, overall eye health, and general medical history. Pregnancy and breastfeeding can temporarily shift the prescription, so treatment is usually postponed. People with very high refractive errors may fall outside the ideal range for LASIK and benefit from a different approach.

Because candidacy depends on objective measurements rather than preference alone, the consultation is the deciding step. If LASIK is not suitable, the care team can explain why and outline the options that match the eyes more safely.
sections.procedure_steps:
Treatment begins with anesthetic eye drops, so no injections or needles are involved and the eye is numb throughout. A soft lid holder keeps the eye comfortably open. In the most common modern approach, a femtosecond laser creates a precise corneal flap; some clinics still offer a microkeratome blade technique, while bladeless femtosecond flaps are now standard at many Korean centers.

The surgeon lifts the thin flap to expose the corneal bed. An excimer laser then removes microscopic layers of tissue in a pattern calculated from the pre-operative scans, reshaping the curvature to correct the refractive error. Wavefront-guided and topography-guided settings can refine this pattern for the individual eye. The laser phase itself usually lasts under a minute per eye, and patients focus on a target light while the system tracks tiny eye movements.

The flap is then floated back into position, where it adheres on its own without stitches and seals as it heals. Both eyes are commonly treated in the same session. Afterward the eyes are checked at the slit lamp, protective shields or sunglasses are provided, and aftercare drops and instructions are reviewed before the patient leaves.
sections.recovery_timeline:
Most patients notice clearer vision within a few hours, though some haze, glare, or light sensitivity is normal on the first day. Mild grittiness or a watery sensation may come and go as the surface settles. Resting the eyes, wearing the supplied shields at night, and avoiding rubbing protect the healing flap during the earliest period.

With a three day stay, the schedule generally allows surgery early in the visit and a next-day check to confirm the flap is well positioned and healing as expected. Many people feel comfortable flying home after this follow-up, once the surgeon confirms the eyes are stable. It is sensible to arrange a window of rest rather than scheduling demanding activities immediately after travel.

Prescribed lubricating and antibiotic drops are used for a set period to support healing and manage dryness, which is common in the first weeks and usually eases over time. Screens, reading, and work can typically resume within a day or two, while swimming, eye makeup, and contact sports are paused for longer as advised. Vision often continues to sharpen and stabilize over several weeks, and a final review with a local optometrist back home is recommended.
sections.cost_breakdown:
Pricing for LASIK in Korea generally ranges from 1500 to 4000 US dollars for both eyes treated together, since refractive surgery is quoted per pair rather than per single eye. The position within that range reflects the laser platform used, the degree of customization such as wavefront or topography guidance, the strength of the prescription, and the clinic itself.

A complete quote usually covers the pre-operative screening and corneal scans, the surgery for both eyes, the initial medications, and the early follow-up visits scheduled during the stay. Patients should confirm exactly what is included, whether the consultation fee applies if surgery proceeds, and how any enhancement or fine-tuning procedure would be handled if needed later.

For international visitors it helps to budget alongside treatment for flights, accommodation for the stay, and local transport. Asking for an itemized estimate in writing before committing makes it easier to compare clinics and avoid surprises at the time of payment.
sections.why_korea:
Korea is recognized as a regional center for vision correction, supported by a national framework in which the Ministry of Health and Welfare, KHIDI, and KOIHA promote standards and accreditation for clinics serving international patients. This oversight gives visitors a clearer way to identify centers that meet recognized benchmarks for safety and care.

Femtosecond and excimer laser systems are standard equipment at established Korean refractive centers, and detailed corneal mapping is a routine part of planning rather than an add-on. The country has built considerable experience in high-volume refractive surgery, and many clinics have organized services around the needs of overseas patients, including coordinated scheduling and English-speaking support.

For patients traveling from Africa, the combination of accredited facilities, modern laser technology, transparent and itemized pricing, and the ability to complete screening, surgery, and a first follow-up within a short visit makes Korea a practical destination. A thorough consultation remains the foundation of a safe outcome, confirming that LASIK is the right match before any treatment proceeds.

faqs.a: Numbing drops are placed before treatment, so the eye is anesthetized and most people feel only light pressure rather than pain while the laser works. Some grittiness, watering, or a burning sensation is common for a few hours afterward as the surface settles. These feelings usually ease quickly, and prescribed lubricating drops help keep the eyes comfortable during early healing.

faqs.q: How soon can I fly home after surgery?
faqs.a: With the typical three day stay, surgery is arranged early in the visit and a next-day follow-up confirms the flap is healing well. Many patients fly home after that check once the surgeon agrees the eyes are stable. Flying itself does not harm the eyes, but it helps to rest, use the prescribed drops, and avoid rubbing during the journey.

The laser reshapes vision by removing a microscopic layer of corneal tissue, so the cornea must be thick enough to stay strong and stable afterward. Thickness is measured during screening. If the cornea is too thin for LASIK, the care team may suggest a surface-based or lens-based procedure that achieves correction without compromising structure.

faqs.q: Can both eyes be treated on the same day?
faqs.a: Yes, both eyes are commonly treated in the same session, which is why pricing is given per pair. Treating them together helps vision balance as healing progresses and reduces the number of visits. The surgeon confirms during screening that both eyes are suitable before scheduling them in one appointment.

The reshaping of the cornea is lasting, and many people enjoy stable vision for years. However, the eye can still change with age, including the natural near-focus shift that affects most people later in life, which may eventually call for reading glasses. A stable prescription before surgery and regular check-ups afterward support long-term results.
